word_studies.summary: Judgment, judicial process. From krino (G2919, to separate/judge). 47 NT occurrences. Distinguished from katakrima (condemnation) — krisis is the judicial PROCESS, not necessarily the negative verdict. LXX maps from both mishpat (H4941, verdict/ordinance, 132x) and riyb (H7379, covenant lawsuit, 29x).
word_studies.relevance: The judgment announcement following 'give glory' in Rev 14:7: 'the hour of his krisis is come.' The riyb→krisis bridge (TR-riyb-krisis.md) shows the covenant-lawsuit background: Rev 14:7 invokes God's legal right to judge creation.

word_studies.summary: Good, beautiful, noble, fit. Of uncertain affinity. 91 unique verses across 16 books. Straddles aesthetic (beautiful) and ethical (good) domains. Distinguished from agathos (intrinsically good): kalos emphasizes observable/appreciable excellence — beauty and fitness perceived by others.
word_studies.relevance: The 'kala erga' (beautiful/good works) in Matt 5:16 and 1 Pet 2:12 are the visible conduct that causes others to glorify God. Kalos means the works are aesthetically compelling — attracting glory — not just morally correct.

word_studies.summary: To complete, perfect, consummate. From telos (end/goal). 24 unique verses across 8 books. Primary domains: moral perfection, completing/finishing, consecrating. Used in John 17:4 (teleiosas — 'having finished the work') as the means of glorification.
word_studies.relevance: The completion verb in Christ's glorification model. John 17:4: teleiosas (having completed) explains edoxasa (I have glorified). Work-completion IS glorification. John 4:34 uses the same verb for Christ's life purpose.

word_studies.summary: To finish, complete, execute, discharge. From telos (G5056, end/goal). 26 unique verses. Related to teleioo but emphasizes the ACT of finishing rather than the STATE of perfection. Used in John 19:30 (tetelestai — 'It is finished').
word_studies.relevance: John 19:30's tetelestai ('It is finished') is the cross's counterpart to John 17:4's edoxasa/teleiosas. The dying statement is a final act of glorification — the work is completed, glory is given.

word_studies.summary: To repent, change one's mind. Compound of meta ('change') + noeo ('to think/perceive'). 32 unique verses in 6 books. Fundamentally cognitive/volitional — a deliberate re-orientation of thought and will, not merely emotional remorse. Concentrated in Synoptics, Acts, and Revelation.
word_studies.relevance: Critical for the Rev 16:9 echo: 'they repented not (ou metenoasan) to give him glory.' The refusal to repent IS the refusal to glorify. Rev 9:20-21 is the precursor. Metanoeo's cognitive nature means it involves the re-orientation of mind that enables glory-giving.

word_studies.summary: Work, deed, action. 161 unique verses in 26 books. Three senses: occupation/employment, product/accomplishment, act/deed. First occurrence: Matt 5:16 ('your good works'). In John 17:4, 'the work (ergon) which thou gavest me to do' — Christ's entire redemptive mission is one ergon.
word_studies.relevance: The 'work' that Christ completed in glorifying the Father (John 17:4). The kala erga ('beautiful works') in Matt 5:16 and 1 Pet 2:12 are the conduct that causes others to glorify God. Believers' works are the visible arena of glory-giving.

word_studies.summary: To transform, transfigure. From meta ('change') + morphoo ('to form'). Only 4 NT occurrences: Matt 17:2, Mark 9:2 (Christ's transfiguration), Rom 12:2 ('be ye transformed'), 2 Cor 3:18 ('are changed'). Denotes change of essential form (morphe), not merely outward appearance (schema). Contrasts with metaschematizo (G3345, change of external presentation) and syschematizo (G4964, conform to external pattern).
word_studies.relevance: The transformation verb in 2 Cor 3:18: metamorphoumetha (present passive — 'we are being transformed'). Same verb as Christ's transfiguration — the transformation is real and radical. In 2 Cor 3:18, the transformation is from glory to glory by the Spirit. In Rom 12:2, transformation is contrasted with conformity (syschematizo). This is how glory-giving becomes possible: believers are transformed into glory-bearing image.

word_studies.summary: To behold as in a mirror; or to reflect as a mirror. Hapax legomenon — occurs only in 2 Cor 3:18. From katoptron ('mirror'). Middle voice. The 'beholding vs. reflecting' debate: Meyer and most moderns favor 'beholding as in a mirror' for the middle voice; Chrysostom and others favor 'reflecting as a mirror.' The middle voice in Greek usage favors the subject's own action of looking, not the mirror's function of reflecting.
word_studies.relevance: The critical verb in the beholding-transformation mechanism. If 'beholding': believers look at God's glory and are changed. If 'reflecting': believers mirror God's glory to others. Either way, sustained engagement with divine glory produces transformation. The hapax status means the word's meaning must be determined from context and cognate usage.

word_studies.summary: To believe, trust, have faith. From pistis (G4102, faith). 220 unique verses across 21 books. The internal correlate of confession (homologeo). In Rom 4:20, faith IS described as 'giving glory to God' — belief is not merely intellectual assent but active glory-giving.
word_studies.relevance: Faith as glory-giving. Rom 4:20: Abraham was 'strong in faith, giving glory to God' — the participle dous doxan is circumstantial to pistei. Faith and glory-giving are identified. Num 20:12 confirms the inverse: Moses' unbelief = failure to sanctify/glorify.

word_studies.summary: Fear, terror, reverence. 44 unique verses across 16 books. Companion noun to the first imperative in Rev 14:7 (phobethete). Studied extensively in 3am-05-fear-god.
word_studies.relevance: Companion imperative to 'give glory.' The fear-glory-worship triad in Rev 14:7 represents one reality from three angles. Prior study 3am-05 established that phobos in Rev 14:7 is reverential awe, not terror.

word_studies.summary: To fear, be afraid, have reverence. 90 unique verses across 14 books. The verb form in Rev 14:7: phobethete (V-AOM-2P, aorist passive imperative). Studied in 3am-05-fear-god.
word_studies.relevance: The fear verb in Rev 14:7. The aorist passive imperative phobethete opens the three-part command. Fear precedes glory-giving as the internal orientation.

word_studies.summary: To worship, bow down, prostrate. 54 unique verses in NT. Third imperative in Rev 14:7: proskynesate (V-AAM-2P). Dominant worship verb in the NT.
word_studies.relevance: Third imperative in Rev 14:7. Worship is the behavioral expression of fear (internal) and glory (relational). The command to worship the Creator contrasts with the beast-worship of Rev 13.

word_studies.summary: To give. One of the most frequent OT verbs (1,814 unique verses). LXX maps primarily to didomi (G1325, 1330 co-occurrences). Used in Josh 7:19 (ten lo todah — 'give him confession') and Mal 2:2 (latheth kabod — 'to give glory'). The Hebrew equivalent of the Greek didomi in the 'give glory' construction.
word_studies.relevance: The Hebrew 'give' verb in the OT glory-giving passages. Nathan + kabod + le-YHWH is the Hebrew construction underlying didomi + doxa + dative in the NT.

word_studies.summary: Full knowledge, recognition, discernment. Strengthened form of gnosis (G1108). 20 occurrences. Used in Rom 1:28 where humanity's refusal to retain God 'in knowledge' (en epignosei) parallels their refusal to glorify Him.
word_studies.relevance: Rom 1:28: they did not retain God 'in knowledge' (epignosis). The failure to acknowledge/know God is the cognitive dimension of the failure to glorify. Glory-giving requires genuine knowledge of who God is.

word_studies.summary: Eternal, everlasting. From aion (G165, age). 69 unique verses across 19 books. Qualifies 'gospel' in Rev 14:6 as euangelion aionion. Studied in 3am-04-euangelion-aionion.
word_studies.relevance: Modifies 'gospel' in Rev 14:6: the everlasting gospel. The 'give glory' command is part of this eternal gospel content.

word_studies.summary: Flesh. 130 unique verses across 22 books. Used in Rom 1:24 context where God 'gave them up' to dishonor their bodies. The sarx/soma distinction matters: sarx often carries the sinful-nature sense while soma is the neutral physical body.
word_studies.relevance: Background for Rom 1:24 (dishonoring bodies through lusts of sarx) and the bodily glorification passages. The sarx-soma contrast helps explain why Paul can command glorifying God 'in your body' (soma) while condemning the 'works of the flesh' (sarx).

word_studies.summary: Word, reason, account. 314 unique verses across 24 books. Used in Rom 12:1 as logiken latreian ('reasonable/logical service/worship'). The logos root connects rationality to worship — bodily glorification is 'logical' service.
word_studies.relevance: Rom 12:1: logiken latreian — worship that engages the rational mind. Glory-giving through bodily presentation is described as 'logical' — it follows necessarily from the mercies of God (Rom 12:1a).

word_studies.summary: To buy out of the marketplace, redeem. Compound of ek ('out of') + agorazo (G59, 'to purchase'). Only 4 NT occurrences (Gal 3:13; 4:5; Eph 5:16; Col 4:5). The purchase-redemption vocabulary background for 1 Cor 6:20's agorazo.
word_studies.relevance: Background for the purchase-price argument in 1 Cor 6:20. agorasthete ('you were bought') uses the simpler G59 agorazo, but exagorazo's 'buy out of the marketplace' metaphor enriches the slavery-redemption context that grounds bodily glorification.

word_studies.summary: Heart — the center of thought, will, and emotion. 152 unique verses across 22 books. Used in Rom 1:21 ('their foolish heart was darkened') — the cognitive/volitional failure that underlies the refusal to glorify.
word_studies.relevance: Rom 1:21: the darkening of the foolish heart follows the refusal to glorify. The heart is the locus of both failure (Rom 1:21) and response (Rom 10:9 — 'with the heart man believeth'). Glory-giving begins in the heart.

word_studies.summary: Authority, power, right. 93 unique verses across 16 books. Used in Rev 16:9 ('God which hath authority/power over these plagues') — emphasizing that the plagues are under God's sovereign control, making the refusal to give glory even more culpable.
word_studies.relevance: Rev 16:9: tou echontos ten exousian epi tas plegas tautas — 'the one having authority over these plagues.' The wicked know God has authority over the plagues yet refuse to give glory — making their refusal willful.

word_studies.summary: World, ordered arrangement. 152 unique verses across 20 books. Used in John 17:5 ('the glory which I had with thee before the world was') — Christ's pre-existent glory before the kosmos existed.
word_studies.relevance: John 17:5: Christ's glory predates the kosmos. The 'before the world was' phrase establishes that glory is not a created attribute but belongs to Christ inherently.

word_studies.summary: Blessed, happy, fortunate. Used in Rev 14:13 ('Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord') — the blessedness that results from responding to the first angel's call to give glory.
word_studies.relevance: Rev 14:13 pronounces a blessing on those who die in the Lord — the outcome for those who heed the first angel's call to fear, give glory, and worship.

word_studies.summary: Testimony, witness. The first angel's message is itself a testimony — a witness to God's claim on creation.
word_studies.relevance: Background for the gospel-as-testimony framework. The 'everlasting gospel' of Rev 14:6 functions as God's witness/testimony to the world, demanding the response of glory-giving.

parallels.connection_type: vocabulary + structural — fear God + judgment; Ecclesiastes is the OT template the first angel restates universally
vocabulary — fear + glorify + worship + judgment; same cluster repeated post-judgment
verbal echo — dote auto doxan (14:7 imperative) vs. dounai auto doxan (16:9 infinitive); same lexemes, shift from command to refusal
vocabulary — edokan doxan to theo (11:13 positive response) using same didomi+doxa construction
vocabulary + structural — heavenly pattern of creation-grounded glory-giving
idiom — 'give glory' as confession/judicial oath formula; sim kabod la-YHWH parallels dote doxan
vocabulary + structural — latheth kabod lishmi; curse for not giving glory parallels plagues in Rev 16
vocabulary — habuw la-YHWH kabod; the call to give glory and worship
vocabulary — creation language 'made heaven and earth and sea' echoes the Sabbath commandment
structural — same logic: Creator commands repentance because judgment is appointed
verbal inversion — edoxasan (glorified not) inverts dote doxan (give glory); same doxa root
vocabulary — 'changed their glory' (Psa 106:20) parallels 'changed the glory' (Rom 1:23)
vocabulary — 'my people have changed their glory' echoes the glory-exchange pattern
structural + thematic — Moses' shining face is the OT background for the beholding-transformation mechanism
vocabulary — conformed to the image (eikon) of the Son; same image-transformation trajectory
vocabulary — teleioo in both; Christ's life-purpose statement anticipates the glorification declaration
vocabulary — tetelestai (G5055 teleo) as the cross's counterpart to John 17:4's completion
thematic — glorify God in approaching Him; same obligation to sanctify/glorify in the divine presence
structural — sixth trumpet refusal to repent anticipates fourth plague refusal to give glory
verbal repetition — 'repented not' repeated under fifth plague; escalating refusal
